quote: Yeah. Sounds good. I'd love to help you out with that.
stewart_read: Nick has just disclosed a warm referral from an active Ion customer — the highest-trust opening a setter can receive. The rep's response is a generic acknowledgment that immediately pivots to data collection ('So, yeah, we do have referral bonuses... I just need some basic information from you'). The referral energy — why Nick is calling, what his father-in-law told him, what Nick is hoping to learn — is never explored. Strategic read: the rep may have been moving efficiently to qualify before investing time. Reactive read: the rep missed the single best rapport anchor on the call. The reactive read holds — there was no later move that picked this up.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was deliberately deferring emotional depth until after basic qualification was confirmed — a reasonable efficiency choice on an unverified lead.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the 00:15 line and ask: 'What did Nick just hand you?' The referral is a trust gift. A single follow-up — 'What did your father-in-law tell you about it?' — would have surfaced Nick's actual motivation, deepened the verify.interest_in_solar beat, and created a natural emotional anchor for the pitch. The rep can be efficient AND curious; these aren't in conflict.

quote: I am tied into one of those, like, solar farms where they give you, like, a percentage of the, you know, they're kind of associated with Excel sort of. I I mean but, like, you get part of your energy is being pulled from a solar farm. Is that I'm sure you've heard of those setups.
stewart_read: Nick is flagging a genuine complexity — a community solar subscription — and explicitly asking whether the rep is familiar with it ('Is that I'm sure you've heard of those setups'). The rep's response ('Okay. Yes. Sounds good. No problem. I'll go ahead and put that down here.') is a social acknowledgment, not an informational one. The rep doesn't probe what this means for Nick's bill, his current offset percentage, or whether it affects the design. This is a knowledge gap: the rep either doesn't know how community solar interacts with rooftop solar, or doesn't know to flag it for the closer. Either way, the closer walks in blind.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep correctly recognized this as closer-tier content and avoided scope creep by not engaging technically — a defensible scope-discipline move.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis has real weight here — the rep shouldn't be explaining community solar mechanics. But the correct scope-discipline move is not silence; it's a bridge: 'That's actually something our specialist will want to know about — it affects how we size the system. I'll make sure that's in the notes.' That keeps scope clean AND flags the complexity for the closer. Manager should use this moment to distinguish 'don't answer technical questions' from 'don't acknowledge technical complexity.'

quote: You can typically get those in the mail or online?
stewart_read: The rep collects the bill format (online) and moves to delivery method. The average monthly bill amount — described in the schema as 'THE anchor for the entire pitch' — was never asked. The rep has Nick's utility company (Xcel), his roof age, his credit, and his address, but has no bill number to anchor the pitch emotionally. The bill collection beat here is purely logistical: get the document, not anchor the pain.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have intended to capture the bill amount from the document itself once received, rather than asking verbally — a reasonable efficiency choice if the document is coming.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis is plausible but incomplete. The schema's bill_anchoring principle is 'capture + flip' — the bill number is the emotional lever, not just a design input. Even if the document is coming, asking 'Do you have a rough sense of what you're paying Xcel each month?' takes ten seconds and gives the rep something to anchor on right now. Manager should ask the rep: 'What would you have said differently if Nick had told you he was paying $300 a month versus $150?' If the rep doesn't have an answer, the flip concept needs direct coaching.

quote: And then, from there, we just need a a good time. We can have a local specialist drop by and kinda go over everything with you.
stewart_read: The rep moves to scheduling before the bill has been received, then at 07:10 makes the confirmation explicitly conditional: 'once I get that bill from you, I'll just send you a confirmation text or email.' This means the appointment has no confirmation anchor until the bill arrives. If Nick doesn't send the bill — he's at work, said it would 'take a little bit to dig that up' — there is no confirmation text, and the appointment drifts. Additionally, the button-up is missing the 'is that fair' verbal handshake (schema: button_up.is_that_fair_handshake) and the no-driving/at-computer anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that a warm referral with a cooperative customer doesn't need the full button-up protocol — that the relationship trust substitutes for procedural anchors.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis doesn't hold: the schema's button-up elements exist precisely because warm customers no-show too. Manager should walk the rep through two specific fixes: (1) send the confirmation text immediately after the call with the Saturday 2PM slot locked, and separately follow up for the bill — don't gate the confirmation on the bill; (2) add the 'is that fair?' handshake before hanging up. These are 30-second additions that materially affect show rate.

quote: Yeah. It's relatively new. I would say 2020 I'd have to double check. It's only been about two years since it's been replaced since the hail damage. So I think it's about two years old.
stewart_read: Nick volunteers that the roof was replaced due to hail damage approximately two years ago — meaning it's a clean, recent asphalt shingle roof with no re-roof barrier to solar installation. This is a green-light signal: no roof age DQ, no cross-sell complexity, and a customer who has already navigated an insurance claim process (suggesting familiarity with home improvement projects). The rep acknowledges it ('Okay. Awesome. Sounds good.') and moves on. The signal was received but not anchored — the rep could have used this to build confidence: 'Perfect — a newer roof is actually ideal for solar, it means the system goes on clean and you're not looking at a re-roof before install.'
coaching_implication: This is a low-stakes coaching moment but a good teaching example for enthusiasm signals. The roof age is a qualifier green-light AND a rapport moment — Nick just told the rep something that makes his home a better candidate. Acknowledging that explicitly ('that's actually great news for the install') costs nothing and builds Nick's confidence that he's a good fit. Manager can use this to introduce the concept of enthusiasm signals as anchoring opportunities, not just data points.

quote: Probably afternoon. I think my wife would wanna be there. She works in a Saturday morning. So Sure. Afternoon is probably better than
stewart_read: Nick self-includes his wife without being prompted — the rep had already confirmed Tamara is on the title at 04:12, and Nick is now voluntarily scheduling around her availability. The rep handles this correctly: schedules for Saturday afternoon to accommodate her work schedule, books a 2PM slot that works for both. This is the right protocol outcome. The rep did not need to invoke the spouse-inclusion script because Nick did the work himself — but the rep's earlier title question (04:06) set up the conditions for this to happen naturally.
coaching_implication: Surface this as a positive example in the 1-on-1. The title question at 04:06 is what made this possible — it's a clean illustration of why the verify.title_co_owners beat matters. The rep can see the direct line from 'is there anyone else on the title' to 'my wife would wanna be there.' Use this to reinforce the verify sequence as setup, not checkbox.

quote: So, yeah, we do have referral bonuses. So, yeah, and it doesn't look we have, like, look doesn't look like we have anything pulled up here on our end, so I just need some basic information from you.
stewart_read: The rep mentions the referral bonus briefly but immediately pivots to data collection. The referral bonus is a legitimate value signal for Nick — it means his father-in-law benefits from this call — but it's dropped in a subordinate clause and never returned to. This is a micro-trade opportunity that was half-executed: the rep named the bonus but didn't use it as a commitment device or a reason for Nick to stay engaged through the verify process.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that Nick was already engaged enough that the bonus didn't need emphasis — a reasonable read on a warm referral.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis is plausible. This is a secondary coaching point, not a primary gap. If the manager has time, the question to ask the rep is: 'Did you come back to the referral bonus at any point?' If not, a brief mention at button-up ('And once your appointment sits, your father-in-law gets his referral credit') would have closed the loop and given Nick an additional reason to show.

quote: yeah, once I get that bill from you, I'll just send you a, confirmation text or email with the date and time of the meeting.
stewart_read: Two button-up protocol gaps in a single closing sequence: (1) the confirmation is gated on the bill arriving rather than sent immediately to lock the appointment, and (2) the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake — schema-required as predictive of show rate — is absent. The call ends with 'Alright. Sounds great. Thanks for your help' from Nick, which is warm but not a verbal commitment to the specific time and terms. The rep also skips the text-after-call tieback requirement — the confirmation text, when it arrives, will be generic rather than personalized to anything Nick said.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that gating the confirmation on the bill creates a natural follow-up touchpoint that keeps Nick engaged — a deliberate sequencing choice rather than a protocol miss.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis doesn't hold: the schema is explicit that the confirmation text should be sent after the call, and that the 'is that fair?' handshake is predictive of show rate. Manager should walk the rep through a two-step fix: send the Saturday 2PM confirmation immediately after hanging up (don't wait for the bill), and add 'is that fair?' before the close. The bill follow-up is a separate action item, not a gate on the confirmation.
